title: Za Beasto Sequel Theory Shinji manning up and honorifics
display_title: Za Beasto Sequel Theory Shinji manning up and honorifics
wikitext:
[]: Za Beasto, Sequel Theory, Shinji manning up and honorifics             Quote Post by Jayfive » Mon Oct 17, 2011 7:46 am All questions regarding 2.22 1) Is 'Za Beasto' actually Japanese or Engrish? 2) Kaworu's 'this time' line in the english dub does not in fact have the 'this time' bit - its simply something like "i give you my solemn vow that I will make you truly happy". Surely if sequel theory had credence, the dub team (presuming they liaise with Khara) would have been imformed to keep it in, if not informed why. 3) Before jumping into Shogouki to give Zeruel a kicking, Shinji has the scene with Gendo thats a mirror image of the initial meeting in 1.11 (or NGE episode 1 if you prefer). Shinji shouts at his dad, Gendo gasps. In the original Japanese is it something to do with the language/honorifics used that surprises/shocks Gendo or simply that Shinji is showing some backbone? 4) speaking of honorifics, would it have been *so* bad to have them in the dub? Most people watching Eva understand the basics of them and its a good opportunity for those that dont to learn. Misato's line as she watches Unit-1 give Zeruel a swift crack to the face is so much better as 'Shinji-kun' and not 'its Shinji!'. Likewise Shinji yell of 'misato-san' shortly afterwards. I dont believe that dubs should neccessary wipe all evidence of the film's country of origin.

[]:             Quote Post by icomeanon4 » Mon Oct 17, 2011 8:21 am 1) "Za" is obviously meant to be "the," and "beast-o" obviously has an "-o" because it sounds unnatural in Japanese to end a word with a consonant other than n. It's Engrish. 2) Sequel theory has already been all but completely discredited. 3) They both go together, I imagine, and having already seen question #4 I can see where you're going with this. 4) Would it have been *so* bad? One word: YEEEEEEESSSS. English does not use Japanese honorifics. Translations are not meant to assist in learning aspects of the other language; that's what watching it in the other language is for. Including honorifics in a dub would wreck the goal of translating: to carry the intent of the original language into the language of the audience as fully as possible, and in a way that assumes no prior knowledge of the original language. Besides, we have ways in English of doing the exact same thing that honorifics do. For example, if you call a police officer "man" or "dude" as opposed to "Mr." or "Sir," you're probably being disrespectful. Literal translation does not preserve the original intent; it actually obfuscates it because the exact wording doesn't do the same thing in the new language as it did in the old. And besides, those examples you gave would not sound better in English. Not by a long shot. I dont believe that dubs should neccessary wipe all evidence of the film's country of original.

[]:             Quote Post by Jayfive » Mon Oct 17, 2011 8:48 am Sequel theory has already been all but completely discredited.

[]:             Quote Post by RyienKu » Mon Oct 17, 2011 11:47 am icomeanon4 wrote: 4) Would it have been *so* bad? One word: YEEEEEEESSSS. English does not use Japanese honorifics. Translations are not meant to assist in learning aspects of the other language; that's what watching it in the other language is for. Including honorifics in a dub would wreck the goal of translating: to carry the intent of the original language into the language of the audience as fully as possible, and in a way that assumes no prior knowledge of the original language. Besides, we have ways in English of doing the exact same thing that honorifics do. For example, if you call a police officer "man" or "dude" as opposed to "Mr." or "Sir," you're probably being disrespectful. Literal translation does not preserve the original intent; it actually obfuscates it because the exact wording doesn't do the same thing in the new language as it did in the old. And besides, those examples you gave would not sound better in English. Not by a long shot.

[]:             Quote Post by Trajan » Mon Oct 17, 2011 1:38 pm I HATE it when weeaboos use honorifics in fan-fiction and stuff. So using them in a dub is out of the question. It's a distinctly Japanese thing and should remain so.

[]:             Quote Post by Jayfive » Mon Oct 17, 2011 2:21 pm Crikey, I didn't think it would be something people felt strongly about. I simply mentioned it in terms of showing that the characters are Japanese or are at least supposed to be speaking it and we're hearing it as english. Funny fan-fiction is mentioned because the only place ive seen itd done is the fan-translated scans of the manga. Dont know whether its used in the official translations of the main manga, raising project, angelic days, campus apocalypse etc. And on here. But in a jokey way. It's "country of origin."

[]:             Quote Post by Rodeo » Mon Oct 17, 2011 7:03 pm Honorifics can be useful if you want to give the dialog an exotic, foreign flavor. In Eva's case I don't think that would be appropriate at all, we're talking about a sci-fi mecha anime, not some ancient period piece. And if you need evidence for the films country of origin, how about all those weird Japanese-sounding names and the constant allusions to a futurist Tokyo?

[]:             Quote Post by Aiko Heiwa » Mon Oct 24, 2011 5:23 am Rodeo wrote: And if you need evidence for the films country of origin, how about all those weird Japanese-sounding names and the constant allusions to a futurist Tokyo?

[]:             Quote Post by Jayfive » Mon Oct 24, 2011 11:57 am How come a whole load of posts have disappeared? Has the thread been split?

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Mon Oct 24, 2011 2:05 pm Jayfive wrote: How come a whole load of posts have disappeared? Has the thread been split?

[]:             Quote Post by Jayfive » Tue Oct 25, 2011 3:11 am Thanks, I did look for a split thread - mustve had a brainfart or something and missed it

[]:             Quote Post by Xacebans » Mon Oct 31, 2011 2:25 pm I don't think it's right to keep honorifics in a translation. Japanese honorifics are part of the Japanese language and don't belong in English. When a film or show is translated from Japanese to English, it is so that the audience can fully understand what is being said in Japanese. Keeping the Japanese honorifics is only a partial translation, and I believe that if you're going to translate something you should do it in a manner that somebody other than geeky anime fans understand, especially in the case of a feauture film such as the Rebuild movies.

[]:             Quote Post by Viking » Tue Nov 15, 2011 6:44 pm Well, I'm not a native English speaker ( and I suppose you already figured that out ), but I assume that even in English there are words that could be used instead of the various honorifics. In the Italian dub for instance, when Shinji call for "Misato-san" during the battle with Zeruel, the line has been translated with the Italian equivalent of "Miss Misato". Sometimes honorifics have been completely dropped, like "Shinji-kun" that simply became "Shinji". By the way, I'm curious about the use of the word "father" ( Otosan if I remember correctly ). In English there are the two words "father" and "dad" that are used depending on the context, but in Japanese? Is it similar or is completely different? Bye

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Tue Nov 15, 2011 7:59 pm Viking wrote: Well, I'm not a native English speaker ( and I suppose you already figured that out ), but I assume that even in English there are words that could be used instead of the various honorifics. In the Italian dub for instance, when Shinji call for "Misato-san" during the battle with Zeruel, the line has been translated with the Italian equivalent of "Miss Misato". Sometimes honorifics have been completely dropped, like "Shinji-kun" that simply became "Shinji".

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Tue Nov 15, 2011 8:21 pm symbv wrote: "Father" is more formal and "Dad" more casual, right? This in Japanese could be "Otosan" and "Papa" but there is also a word "Chichi" that is only used as a "humble honorific" to refer to your own father when you are talking to a person outside your circle. The concept of "internality" and "externality" in different social circles is VERY IMPORTANT in Japanese language. You never refer to your own father as "otosan" when you are talking to an outsider, but you will have to refer to his father as his "otosan", for example.

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Tue Nov 15, 2011 8:33 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: Strict mothers would insist upon being address by "Ma'am" in the same manner. I don't know if there's a Japanese equivalent for any of this, and of there is, whether or not it plays into the movie.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Tue Nov 15, 2011 9:13 pm I see. So that's why the 2.22 English subs use the term "Captain Katsuragi" rather than "ma'am". I always thought "Captain Katsuragi" would have sounded cool anyway because phonetically both words start with the same sound in the English language. I forget if that term was used in the English dubs of the film, though.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Tue Nov 15, 2011 9:15 pm symbv wrote: In English, you ask "You are not coming?" and if I answer "Yes" I guess it will be taken as "Yes, I am coming" ?

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Tue Nov 15, 2011 9:21 pm gatotsu911 wrote: This is actually kind of a weird scenario in terms of everyday spoken English because in this context "yes" could alternately mean "yes, I am coming" or "yes, I am not coming"; the phrasing makes it ambiguous. Of course, technically speaking an isolated "yes" or "no" is not a gramatically acceptable answer to that question the way you phrased it; the appropriate answer would be "I am coming" or "I am not coming".

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Tue Nov 15, 2011 9:24 pm symbv wrote: I am not sure if my example is the best example to illustrate that, but my point is still the same: the way Yes is used is different from the way Hai is used in their respective language.
